text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Patel
St Cuthbert’s NHS Neurology Clinic
2nd Floor, Outpatients, St Cuthbert’s Hospital, Westgate, Leeds LS1 4AB

Dear Dr Patel,

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1990, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9012456732
Flat 12, 5 Brookfield Way, Leeds, LS6 2ER

Diagnoses:

Recurrent seizures of unclear classification; prior normal MRI (reported 2017). EEG pending repeat review.

Current Medication:

Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily

Plan:

- Increase Lamotrigine to 100 mg twice daily; check trough level in 2–3 weeks
- Arrange urgent sleep-deprived EEG and repeat safety counselling
- Occupational health referral regarding manual handling and shift pattern adjustments
- Safety advice around bathing, heights, operating machinery, and driving in line with DVLA guidance
- Follow up in 8–10 weeks or sooner if further events

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports a gradual worsening over the past six months, coinciding with working in a care environment with irregular breaks and frequent patient transfers. He describes events now occurring roughly every 3–4 weeks, most often arising in the latter part of the night, typically after several days of broken sleep and cumulative fatigue. His partner has noted a loud groan followed by full body stiffening and jerking for approximately 60–90 seconds, with tongue biting on two occasions and post-event confusion lasting 20–30 minutes. There has been one daytime event in the last month after consecutive long shifts and missed meals. He feels more drained between episodes and is worried about performance at work.

There have been no new neurological focal symptoms between episodes. No recent intercurrent illness or alcohol binges; caffeine intake modest. Medications are taken as prescribed, but timing sometimes drifts on night shifts. No missed Lamotrigine doses reported in the week preceding the last two events.

On examination today, observations were stable. Neurological examination was unremarkable. Bloods earlier this year (FBC, U&E, LFT, B12, folate, glucose, thyroid) were within reference ranges. We will repeat bloods alongside the Lamotrigine level after dose adjustment.

Given the clear pattern of deterioration and the clustering of events in the latter portion of the night in the context of sleep disruption and physical workload, I have advised an increase in Lamotrigine to 100 mg twice daily and reinforced adherence to consistent dosing times. We discussed sleep hygiene and minimising triggers, particularly cumulative fatigue. I have also recommended an occupational health assessment to consider more regular breaks and limiting heavy patient transfers while control is being re-established.

He has been given safety advice including avoiding lone bathing and swimming, caution with hot surfaces, and not working at heights. We discussed driving regulations; he understands he should follow DVLA guidance and will contact the agency as appropriate based on recent events.

I will review him in 8–10 weeks following the medication adjustment and investigations, or earlier if there is any further escalation.

With best wishes,

entities.text: events now occurring roughly every 3–4 weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "events now occurring roughly every 3–4 weeks." This indicates a frequency of about one seizure every 3 to 4 weeks. The mention of "one daytime event in the last month" is consistent with that rate and does not imply a higher frequency. No cluster pattern with counts per cluster is defined. Therefore, the normalized label is 1 per 3 to 4 week.
seizure_frequency_number: events now occurring roughly every 3–4 weeks
reference: Seizure in second half of the night

flaws.description: Gold (1 per 3 to 4 week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
